chunks.title: 1. Course Information SPRING 2026
chunks.content:
Course Number: ISEN 410
Course Title: Advanced Engineering Economy
Section: 500 (41679)
Meeting Times: On campus: Tuesday/Thursday 3:55PM – 5:10PM
Location: On-campus: ETB 2026
Credit Hours: 3
Course Delivery: On-campus: face-to-face in ETB 2026 with Canvas LMS

chunks.title: 2. Instructor Details
chunks.content:
Instructor: Donald R. Smith Ph.D., PE Senior Associate Professor
Office: ETB 4038
Phone: Office Phone: 979 458-2346 or E-mail
E-Mail: dr-smith@tamu.edu
Office Hours: T/Th 1:30-3:30 Other - by appointment: The instructor will remain after each class for student consultations as needed.

TA/Grader Prashant Dhakal

Office Hours: By appointment (Email)

chunks.title: 3. Catalog Course Description:
chunks.content:
ISEN 410 Advanced Engineering Economy
Credits: Three Lecture Hours.
Principles of economic equivalence; borrowing, lending, and investing; establishing minimum attractive rate of return; replacement analysis; capital budgeting; uncertainty analysis; decision trees.

Note: capital budgeting, uncertainty analysis and decision tree topics have been dropped pending departmental approval of a revise ISEN 410 course description.

Prerequisites: Grade of C or better in ISEN 210 or ISEN 302; junior or senior classification.

Restrictions: None

chunks.title: 4. Special Course Designation(s)
chunks.content: None.

chunks.title: 5. Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content:
CONTEMPORARAY ENGINEERING ECONOMICS – Seventh Edition. Copyright 2023.
Chan S. Park, Department of Industrial and Systems Engineering; Auburn University. Pearson Publishing.
Full text: ISBN-13: 978-0-13-763329-6
Digital version (special format for ISEN 410)

• The materials for this course are delivered online by Pearson.
• To purchase online with a major credit card or PayPal, go to:
https://console.pearsoned.com/enrollment/yovctu.
• If you need to purchase through your bookstore, you may buy an access code with this ISBN:
9780135599747. Redeem the access code by visiting link:
https://console.pearsoned.com/enrollment/yovctu.
• Once there, sign in or create a new Pearson account.
• ISEN 410: ADVANCED ENGINEERING ECONOMY
• Don Smith
• Access Code ISBN: 9780135599747
• Student Online Purchase Price: $20.40

• Course Start Date: 05-Jan-2026
• Subscription Length: 6 months from student registration

Software (Excel-based for the course)
1. Microsoft Office suite specifically, Word, Excel, and PowerPoint.
2. SciLab (free open source) or MATLAB. A lecture will present details before using either MATLAB or the free version, SciLab. This software is needed for text, chapter 7.
https://www.scilab.org/ Windows or Mac OS x – version 6.0.2 is OK.

Note: Excel skills are assumed for this course!

chunks.title: 6. Grade Determination
chunks.content:
Graded items: Three major exams spaced throughout the course plus recorded attendance per each class.

| Exam | Points | Percent of Grade |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 100 | 28.57% |
| 2 | 100 | 28.57% |
| 3 | 150 | 42.85% final exam 2-hr time limit |
| | 350 | |

chunks.title: Point Totals:350
chunks.content: Point Totals:350

chunks.title: 6.1 Late Work Policy
chunks.content: Missing a major exam without a valid and verified reason will result in a 0 grade for that exam.

chunks.title: 6.2 Exam Dates Spring 2026: TENTATIVE.
chunks.content:
1 February 26 100 points
2. April 7 100 Points
3 May 4 150 Points (Final Exam):
350 Total points

In the event of disruptions to the course schedule the dates for exam 1 and 2 may be altered with written notification to all students (email and Campus notification.)
Note: The university determines when final letter grades will appear in Howdy

chunks.title: 7. Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
• Ability to formulate and evaluate engineering economy-type problems
• Ability to demonstrate Excel modeling of a wide variety of engineering economy-type problems
• Ability to model and correctly interpret the engineering economic analysis of industrial-type problems and investment alternatives for implementation assuming certainty.

chunks.title: 8. Course Topics
chunks.content:
Chapter 1: Engineering Economic Decisions
1.1 Role of Engineers in Business and Project management
1.2 What makes the Engineering Economic Decision Difficult?
1.3 Large-Scale engineering projects
1.4 Common types of Strategic Engineering Economic Decisions Difficult
1.5. Fundamental Principles of Engineering Economic Decisions
Assigned problems

Chapter 3: Interest Rates and Economic Decisions
3.1 Interest: The cost of money
3.2 Economic Equivalence
3.3 Development of Formulas for Equivalence Calculations
3.4 Unconventional Equivalence Calculations
Assigned problems

Ch 4: Understanding Money Management and its Management
4.1 Market Interest Rates
4.2 Equivalence Calculations with Interest Rates
4.3 Omit
4.4 Changing Interest Rates
4.5 Debt Management – Loans
4.6 Investing with Financial Assets – Bonds
Assigned Problems

EXAM 1: Chapters 1,3, & 4 100 Points – February 26, 2026

Chapter 5: Present-Worth Analysis with Excel Models
5.1 Describing Project Cash Flows – Loans, Single Cash Flow, Mutually Exclusive Cash Flows
5.2 Initial Project Screening Methods – Payback and Discounted Payback
5.3 Net Present Value Analysis with Excel Model
5.4 Future Worth Analysis with Excel Model
5.5 Comparing Mutually Exclusive Projects – Excel Model
Assigned Problems
Chapter 6: Annual Equivalent-Worth Analysis
6.1 Annual Worth Analysis with Excel Model
6.2 Capital Costs vs. Operating Costs
6.3 Applications of Annual Worth Analysis – Excel based models
Assigned Problems

Chapter 7: Rate-of-Return Analysis – with Excel models
7.1 Rate of Return – meaning and comparison to NPV
7.2 Simple and Nonsimple investments – Mixed and Pure Investments
7.3 Tests for Mixed and Pure Investments – Excel model with Plots:
7.4 Net Investment criteria and Modified Rate of Return Method, Incremental Investment
Fisherian Intersection for Mutually Exclusive Alternatives – Excel Model
Assigned Problems

Exam 2: Chapters 5,6, and 7 – 100 Points – April 7, 2026

Chapter 9: Depreciation and Corporate Taxes – with Excel Models
9.1 Asset Depreciation – Economic and Accounting Depreciation
9.2 Factors Inherent in Asset Depreciation – Cost Bases, Useful Life and Salvage Value
9.3 Book Depreciation: Straight Line, Declining Balance, Sum-of-the Years
9.4 Tax Depreciation – MACRS method
9.5 Omit
9.6 Repairs & Improvements to productive Assets
9.7 Corporate Taxes – 21% flat corporate tax
Assigned Problems

Chapter 10: Developing Project Cash Flows – Excel Models
10.1 Estimating Cash Flows for Simple and Complex Projects
10.2 Incremental Esch Flow After Tax models
10.3 Developing After Tax Models: Working Capital, Borrowed Funds, cost only models and multiple project Cash Flows – Excel based analysis

Exam 3; Final Exam – 150 Points
Monday May 4, 2026 1:00 – 3: PM. (Time/Date fixed by the university).

chunks.title: Grade Discussion Policy
chunks.content: Any concerns regarding a grade received on any graded material for On-Campus students must be discussed within ONE day of the return of the graded material. No grade will be changed beyond the ONE-day limit. Any requests made after that deadline will not be considered. Grades will be maintained in Canvas and on my office computer.

chunks.title: 8.1 Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
• Missing a major exam date must be fully documented to the satisfaction of the instructor and is to be communicated within one or more days of the exam date. In this situation, a different exam may be administered. Note: job interviews are not valid reasons for missing a scheduled exam!
• Late submission of required work will not be accepted.

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is exempted from the late work policy. Late work policies must link to Student Rule 7.

chunks.title: 8.2 Other Information
chunks.content: The final is scheduled by the university and cannot be changed. Put the time/date on your calendar. Asking to take the final at some other time will not be honored except in most unusual and documented situations.

chunks.title: 12. AI Statement
chunks.content: Since Artificial Intelligence (AI) is becoming a popular element in higher education this course does not incorporate Al methods as the course is heavily involved with the formulation and analysis of engineering economy type problems.

chunks.title: Academic Integrity Statement and Policy (Course Specific)
chunks.content: The Industrial and Systems Engineering Department has adopted a “no tolerance” position concerning violations of the Aggie Honor Code. In this course, I will request the Aggie Honor Office to assign a grade of F* for any violation of the Aggie Honor Code, no matter how minimal the violation may be.

chunks.title: Attendance Grade Component
chunks.content: Par of the student's grade is attendance-based (7% of the final grade.)

chunks.title: 21. Texas A&M College Station
chunks.content: Students needing a listening ear can contact University Health Services at 979.458.4584. Call 911 or visit your nearest emergency room if you are currently experiencing a life-threatening situation or if your safety is at risk. 24-hour emergency help is also available through the 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline (988) or at 988lifeline.org.
